title: Nex
summary: There are places in Ishredon where beauty is immediate, and others where it must be learned. Nex belongs to the latter and rewards the patient observer far more than the hasty one. markdown:
# Nex

![Nex Mountain Hold](/lore/images/nex.png)

There are places in Ishredon where beauty is immediate, and others where it must be learned. Nex belongs to the latter and rewards the patient observer far more than the hasty one. A traveler approaching from the lowlands may at first think only of stone, weather, and inconvenience: steep roads, hard ridges, cold lochs, sudden mists, and settlements that seem to have chosen cliffs merely to test the sincerity of visitors. Stay longer, however, and a different understanding emerges. Nex is not harsh for want of grace. It is disciplined, and its grace is built into things that do not yield.

This is the province of mountains, cave systems, underground cities, deep valleys, and old engineered works whose makers expected them to outlive dynasties. Dwarves and gnomes form the greater part of its population, though many others pass through its roads and halls for trade, study, or employment. It is a land where doors weigh more, promises last longer, and people are often judged less by their charm than by the precision of their work. Such a system has disadvantages for the charming and great advantages for everyone else.

To outsiders, Nex is often reduced to metal, gems, and clever devices, which is rather like reducing Landred to bread or Ostrengarr to song. The wealth is real, certainly. So too are the foundries, vaults, mines, liftworks, survey schools, and subterranean markets. Yet what distinguishes Nex is not simply what it produces, but the philosophy behind its making. Here, utility is not regarded as the enemy of beauty. A bridge may be admired because it has stood three centuries without fuss. A lamp may be loved because it is repairable. A hall may be judged noble not because it is lavish, but because every angle in it was chosen well.

Nex does not stand apart from the rest of Ishredon so much as beneath it in every practical sense. Landred feeds many of its holds. Ostrengarr supplies hardwoods, resins, dyes, and seaborne goods difficult to obtain in the high country. Auromass buys mechanisms, fittings, weights, and technical minds with relentless enthusiasm. Even Fildren, ruinous though it is, draws northern expeditions from Nex whenever old shafts, lost strongholds, or abandoned workings are rumored beneath the snow.

## Of the Character of the Province

Nex rises in broken mountains and old stone country where the roads wind through passes, along loch sides, and beneath slopes marked with quarry scars and narrow terraced settlements. On the surface one finds weather-beaten villages, smoke from high chimneys, stocky halls, toll tunnels, cranes over ravines, and fortified gate towns that guard access to richer depths below. Beneath the mountains lies the truer body of the province: excavated roads, reservoir caverns, lantern galleries, engine chambers, deep marketplaces, archive vaults, and the long inhabited under-realms from which Nex draws much of its strength.

The dwarves of Nex are famed for stone, metal, and endurance, which is accurate so far as it goes and incomplete in all the ways that matter. Their craftsmanship is inseparable from custom. Measure, inheritance, reputation, and proof carry tremendous weight. A thing made well reflects not only on its maker, but on teacher, lineage, guild, and hold. Gnomes, meanwhile, lend the province much of its nimbleness: instruments, precise surveying, clockwork systems, experimentation, and an attitude toward mechanism that is at once scholarly and faintly alarming. Between them they have made Nex one of the most indispensable provinces in Ishredon and, not coincidentally, one of the least inclined to hurry for anyone else's convenience.

Magic is treated in Nex much as unstable masonry would be treated: not denied, not worshipped, but approached with caution, measurement, and several layers of backup planning. Old enchantments survive in certain vaults and mechanisms, though even the boldest masters prefer a reliable gear train to a mysterious force whose temperament cannot be documented. It is therefore no surprise that many of the marvels foreigners attribute to sorcery in Nex turn out, on inspection, to be engineering carried to a degree of excellence they have never before witnessed.

The province's politics are as layered as its architecture. Surface lords, clan elders, merchant syndicates, guild masters, hallwardens, and chartered houses all exert influence, though seldom in the same chamber and never without paperwork. Outsiders often complain that Nex is slow to decide. This is untrue. Nex decides at once that a matter requires seven ledgers, four inspections, and a properly witnessed agreement before anyone is trusted with a key.

This method frustrates its neighbors and binds them to it in equal measure. Landred merchants complain about delays while ordering another season of tools. Auromass innovators roll their eyes at guild caution and then ask for finer tolerances. Ostrengarr captains bargain fiercely for Nex metal because they know precisely what it can endure. Such is the privilege of indispensable provinces: they are forgiven much, though rarely gracefully.

## The Great Places of Nex

### Kharad Vey

Kharad Vey is the greatest city of Nex, though to call it a city without qualification is misleading. It is a mountain realm of terraces, gateworks, descending avenues, cavern halls, forge districts, cisterns, bridges, and lower vault neighborhoods lit by disciplined rows of lanterns. Some parts of Kharad Vey stand in daylight upon the mountain face; others disappear into the stone so completely that visitors lose all sense of weather and hour.

The grandeur of Kharad Vey is not theatrical in the southern style. It does not seek to dazzle with frivolity. Its effect is cumulative and absolute. Pillars are vast because they must be. Stairways are broad because centuries of traffic required them. The forge quarter glows like a held sunrise, and the deep halls ring with labor, trade, and the clipped courtesy of people who do not have time to waste explaining obvious matters to foreigners.

### Bronzedeep

If Kharad Vey is the political and civic heart of Nex, Bronzedeep is its industrial lung. Few places in Ishredon can rival it for foundries, ore lifts, smelters, machine yards, and the relentless conversion of raw stone into usable wealth. The air tastes of metal there. Water channels hiss beside heat vents. Entire districts operate according to shift bells and load tallies rather than sunlight.

Travelers fond of cleanliness may not love Bronzedeep, but anyone interested in how the wider continent actually functions ought to see it once. One leaves with a stronger respect for rivets, ore carts, and the people willing to spend their lives beneath a mountain making civilization possible for everyone else.

### Gearhold

The gnomish strong city of Gearhold is a place of workshops, calculation houses, lecture rooms, testing yards, instrument galleries, and alarming enthusiasm for improvement. Clocks, lifts, surveying tools, pumps, precision lenses, drafting rigs, and devices whose purpose is not immediately apparent emerge from Gearhold in steady numbers. Some change the world. Others explode in controlled ways that locals describe as educational.

Gearhold is brighter in spirit than many Nex settlements, though not necessarily easier to understand. Its citizens can converse cheerfully for an hour about counterweights and then forget to explain where the door is.

### Loch Beryl

Set upon a cold and beautiful mountain loch, Loch Beryl is among the more outward-facing settlements of Nex. Traders, fishers, carters, hostel keepers, and stone bargemen mingle there with miners from the interior. The loch itself reflects cliff and cloud with such solemnity that visitors often fall quiet without knowing why.

## Holds, Towns, and Mountain Roads

### Hammercross

A major pass town and gate settlement where caravans regroup, tolls are counted, and nobody passes into richer country without being noticed.

### Slatebridge

Built around a great stone bridge across a ravine, Slatebridge prospers on traffic, quarry trade, and the understated pride of a town that knows its bridge is better than most southern castles.

### Runnel's End

A mining town clinging to a steep valley where ore, rumor, and grievances all travel downward quickly.

### Cold Lantern

A tunnel mouth settlement known for hostels, repair yards, and the last reliable warmth before certain deeper roads.

### Tinvein

A smaller extraction town whose people are practical even by Nex standards, which should tell you something.

### High Sump

Half drainage station, half settlement, High Sump exists because somebody had to live beside the pumps and therefore over time a town happened.

### Fellglass

Known for fine polished stone, cut crystal, and mirror work, Fellglass lends a rare elegance to the harder trades of the province.

### Deepmarket

A subterranean trade quarter and town in its own right, where merchants from three provinces may bargain under one roof and all of them complain about the ventilation.

## Villages and Lesser Stops

### Copper Rise

A hillside village of miners' families, goat keepers, and stubborn terraces hacked into land that did not wish to be terraced.

### Bell Hollow

A small settlement near an old signal chamber where every hour seems to echo longer than it should.

### Flint Mere

One of the rare quieter places in Nex, known for a small lake, disciplined fishing, and stone cottages built as if storms hold personal grudges.

### Mossgate

A cave-mouth village where damp, trade, and pack traffic combine to make every wall seem older than it is.

### Warden's Stair

A stepped road settlement built beside a famously difficult ascent. Mules hate it. Porters respect it. Travelers remember it.

### Ironwake

A smithing village serving nearby roads, ferries, and farmsteads with practical ironwork rather than anything showy.

### Bright Anvil

A compact craft village known for toolmaking, apprenticeships, and the local belief that a badly balanced hammer reflects moral weakness.

### Seven Bolts

A roadside work village named after a repaired gate mechanism that is still discussed with pride generations later.

## Guild Halls, Vaults, and Great Works

### The Hall of Measures

An imposing civic and guild complex where standards of weight, length, fit, and engineering practice are maintained with a seriousness outsiders often mistake for comedy, until something important depends upon accuracy and they cease laughing.

### The Grand Lift of Kharad Vey

A monumental lift system descending through major levels of the capital. To first-time visitors it feels miraculous. To locals it is simply transport and no excuse for standing in the way.

### The Archive Vaults

Storehouses of plans, contracts, genealogies, mining records, repair logs, and civic agreements. Less glamorous than treasure chambers and, in practice, more fiercely defended.

### The Floodwheels

A vast underground water-control system of wheels, sluices, spill chambers, and reservoir gates without which large sections of Nex would become uninhabitable very quickly indeed.

### Emberforge Hall

A major forge complex famous for weapons, ceremonial metalwork, and several feuds so old they now qualify as tradition.

### The Clock Court

A gnomish civic square and testing district where public timekeeping, demonstration machines, and professional rivalry all share the same paving stones.

## Depths, Ruins, and Places Better Entered with a Guide

### The Old Delves

Abandoned lower workings beneath active settlements, full of exhausted shafts, sealed galleries, forgotten tools, and the possibility that not everything left behind was inanimate.

### Whispervault

A disused chamber network whose acoustics carry voices in unsettling ways across great distances. Surveyors insist there is a rational explanation, which I do not dispute and do not enjoy.

### The Broken Span

A collapsed interior bridge over a deep chasm, now traversed only by the desperate, the expert, or those who have been misinformed.

### Runefall Shaft

A legendary deep cut marked by old warning carvings and newer barricades, both of which appear to have been ignored repeatedly over the centuries.

### Stormback Pass

A surface route through high exposed mountains, beautiful when clear and unwise when not, which is most of the time.

### The Hollow Reservoir

An old water chamber partly drained and partly sealed, feared by some engineers and adored by treasure hunters, which is generally a bad sign.

### The Sleeping Doors

Ancient sealed gates in a lower district whose hinges, markings, and history predate the current hold records. Their continued closure is regarded by sensible people as a success.

## Counsel for the Traveler

Come to Nex prepared to climb, to wait, and to be corrected. Bring boots worthy of stone, coin of respectable quality, and a tolerance for being informed that the proper tool, route, or protocol is not the one you had imagined. Pay craftsmen on time, do not mock ledgers, never touch a mechanism unless invited, and resist the southern impulse to call everything underground mysterious when much of it is simply well maintained.

Do not come expecting quick warmth of manner, though you may find deep loyalty once trust is earned. Nex is not an easy province in the social sense, but it is an honest one. Its roads are where they are for reasons. Its halls stand because someone built them correctly. Its people respect competence because incompetence in their country has always had a habit of collapsing from a height. If Landred teaches endurance through labor, Ostrengarr through culture, and Fildren through survival, then Nex teaches the stern and admirable virtue of making things that will still matter after their makers are gone.

*Edward Pennywhistle - Explorer Extraordinaire*
